macro_events.event_summary: Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz again, firing on a vessel attempting to cross — threatening ~20% of global oil flows and LNG shipments.
primary_drivers: Oil supply shock / Brent spike from blocked Hormuz transit (~17-20M bpd choke point)
LNG disruption (Qatar's entire LNG export flows through Hormuz)
Geopolitical risk premium → defense spending, safe-haven flows, tanker rates

beneficiaries.rationale: Large integrated with significant non-Gulf upstream (Permian, Guyana); benefits from Brent spike without the direct Hormuz transit exposure. Every $10 Brent move is material to EPS.
Similar to XOM — US/Permian-weighted production benefits from global price spike; Tengiz volumes route via CPC, not Hormuz.
Pure-play Permian producer with zero Middle East exposure; highest torque to WTI/Brent among large E&Ps.
Scorpio Tankers — product tanker rates spike on Hormuz closure as cargoes reroute and ton-miles explode. Direct rate-sensitivity.
Frontline — VLCC/Suezmax operator; crude tanker day-rates surge on rerouting and scarcity of non-Hormuz crude shipments.
Lockheed — elevated Middle East conflict risk drives near-term missile defense (THAAD, PAC-3) and munitions reorders; clearest defense prime beneficiary.
Cheniere — US LNG export capacity becomes scarcer/more valuable if Qatari cargoes are stranded; spot LNG price spike lifts marketing margins.

losers.rationale: Delta — highest jet fuel sensitivity among legacy carriers; Brent spike directly hits unhedged fuel costs, pressuring margins.
United — similar jet-fuel cost exposure plus international long-haul routes (including Gulf-adjacent) face rerouting and insurance cost hikes.
Southwest — historically hedged but hedge book has thinned; 100% domestic means no fare mix offset from international premium.
Carnival — bunker fuel is a top-3 cost line; also consumer discretionary pullback on recession/oil-shock fears compounds the hit.
Dow Chemical — ethane/naphtha feedstock cost spike plus European ops face gas price surge from lost Qatari LNG; margin compression.
Walmart — low-income consumer gets squeezed by gasoline pump prices (pass-through from Brent), pressuring discretionary basket and traffic.
FedEx — jet fuel + ground diesel costs; express air network has limited ability to pass through a sudden fuel surcharge before contract resets.

headlines: Iran says Strait of Hormuz is closed again as vessel attempting to cross comes under gunfire

macro_events.event_summary: Iran war and $4 gasoline are suppressing US consumer discretionary spending, with concerns about limited time to revive the consumer economy.
primary_drivers: Higher gasoline prices acting as a consumer tax, reducing discretionary income
Geopolitical risk premium in oil prices from Middle East conflict
Shift in consumer wallet from discretionary to staples/energy

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with significant upstream exposure; direct earnings leverage to sustained $80+ crude from Iran conflict risk premium. Refining margins also elevated at $4 gas.
Similar integrated leverage to XOM; every $10/bbl move in Brent adds meaningful EPS. Direct beneficiary of geopolitical premium.
Pure-play US refiner; $4 retail gas implies fat crack spreads. Marathon's retail/Speedway exposure captures pump-level margin.
Consumer trade-down beneficiary — when gas eats discretionary budgets, Walmart gains share from higher-income shoppers and grocery wallet. Historically outperforms in stagflation-lite environments.
Deep-discount retailer benefits from consumer trade-down; fixed price points become more attractive as real incomes compress from gas prices.
Active Iran conflict drives munitions replenishment and supplemental defense appropriations; direct revenue exposure via PAC-3, THAAD, JASSM.

losers.rationale: Cruise operator with double hit: jet/bunker fuel cost inflation and discretionary travel demand compression as consumer wallets shrink. Also highly levered balance sheet.
Same mechanism as CCL — fuel costs and discretionary travel squeeze; Mediterranean itinerary exposure adds geopolitical cancellation risk from Iran conflict.
Darden (Olive Garden, LongHorn) — casual dining traffic is highly sensitive to gas prices; $4 gas historically correlates with negative same-store traffic at middle-income chains.
Consumer discretionary restaurant with premium pricing; at $4 gas the marginal $12 burrito faces demand elasticity, particularly for lower-income cohort.
Southwest has no fuel hedges structurally and domestic leisure exposure; jet fuel tracks crude, and leisure travel demand softens with gas-pinched consumers.
Auto demand — particularly trucks/SUVs that are Ford's profit center — is inversely correlated with gas prices; affordability already stretched and $4 gas accelerates mix shift away from F-150.
Online travel platform levered to discretionary trip bookings; Iran conflict suppresses international travel appetite and higher airfare from fuel costs dampens volume.
Macy's — middle-income apparel/department store is classic casualty when gas acts as a tax on discretionary; already weak consumer, this compounds.

headlines: Iran war, $4 gas, took all the fun out of the U.S. consumer economy. There's only so much time to bring it back

macro_events.event_summary: Trump's Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh is expected to pursue rate cuts, signaling a more dovish Fed regime ahead.
primary_drivers: Lower expected policy rate path → lower discount rate on long-duration assets
Steeper yield curve / lower funding costs for rate-sensitive sectors
Weaker USD expectation on dovish Fed pivot

beneficiaries.rationale: D.R. Horton's volume is directly tied to 30Y mortgage rates; lower policy rate expectations compress mortgage spreads and revive buyer affordability in entry-level housing where DHI dominates.
High-multiple growth name whose valuation is extremely rate-sensitive; retail-favored vehicle for expressing a dovish Fed pivot alongside momentum flows.
Rocket Companies is a pure-play on mortgage origination volume; lower rate expectations trigger refi wave anticipation. Extremely high beta to 10Y yield moves.

losers.rationale: Charles Schwab's cash sweep earnings shrink as short rates fall; net interest revenue is highly exposed to front-end of curve which a dovish chair directly lowers.

headlines: Trump expects his Fed chair nominee to cut interest rates. Here’s how Kevin Warsh might try to do it.

macro_events.event_summary: Post-Iran war commentary frames the US as the world's most secure energy power, implying durable structural demand for US-sourced oil, gas and LNG exports while Middle East supply risk premium compresses.
primary_drivers: US energy export dominance / LNG and crude export infrastructure value rises
Middle East geopolitical risk premium in oil prices compresses, pressuring marginal high-cost producers but benefiting US integrateds with export leverage
Policy tailwind for domestic E&P, pipelines, and LNG terminals as 'strategic assets'

beneficiaries.rationale: Cheniere Energy is the largest US LNG exporter (Sabine Pass, Corpus Christi); a narrative of US as 'most secure energy power' directly validates long-term LNG offtake contracts and expansion economics. Europe/Asia buyers prioritize US supply over Qatari/Gulf routes.
Cheniere Energy Partners owns Sabine Pass liquefaction — pure-play on US LNG export throughput, directly levered to the structural demand shift toward US molecules.
ExxonMobil has the largest Permian footprint post-Pioneer deal plus Golden Pass LNG coming online; benefits from both domestic production premium and export capacity monetization.
Largest US natural gas pure-play producer (Appalachia); direct beneficiary of expanded LNG export narrative tightening domestic gas balances.
Kinder Morgan operates the largest US natural gas pipeline network feeding Gulf Coast LNG terminals; toll-road exposure to rising export volumes without commodity price risk.
Williams Companies' Transco pipeline is the primary artery moving Appalachian gas to Gulf Coast LNG export — direct volume beneficiary of the export-security thesis.
Diamondback Energy is a Permian pure-play; low-cost US barrels become strategically preferred over OPEC supply in a 'energy security' policy frame, supporting premium valuation.

losers.rationale: Halliburton has meaningful Middle East service exposure; if regional reconstruction lags and US policy favors domestic production, international services mix weakens. Also pressured if crude drops on risk-premium unwind.
Occidental has higher breakeven than Permian pure-plays and carries leverage; more vulnerable to any crude price decline from geopolitical premium compression despite US-centric footprint.

headlines: The real outcome of the Iran war: America is now the world’s most secure energy power

macro_events.event_summary: Iran claims the Strait of Hormuz is "under strict control," suggesting no imminent disruption to the critical oil chokepoint, which could ease a geopolitical risk premium in oil ahead of Tesla earnings.
primary_drivers: Reduction in geopolitical risk premium on crude oil
Lower input costs for fuel-sensitive sectors

beneficiaries.rationale: Jet fuel is ~20-25% of operating costs; easing oil risk premium directly improves margin outlook. UAL has high operating leverage to fuel price moves.
Same fuel-cost mechanism as UAL; Delta hedges less than some peers so benefits faster from spot crude relief.
Southwest has historically been fuel-price sensitive; a pullback in Brent from eased Hormuz fears flows to near-term guidance.
Fuel is a major cost line for the Express segment; receding oil risk premium supports margin expectations.

losers.rationale: Integrated major with significant upstream leverage to Brent; an unwind of the Hormuz risk premium pressures crude and E&P earnings expectations.
High-beta US E&P name (Permian-focused but trades with Brent sentiment); geopolitical risk unwinds typically hit OXY harder than majors.

headlines: Dow Jones Futures: Iran Says Hormuz 'Under Strict Control'; Tesla Earnings Due
